_about: Fusion capability smoke tasks modeled after OpenRouter Fusion's public definition: parallel model perspectives, structured judge analysis (consensus / contradictions / partial coverage / unique insights / blind spots), and a grounded final answer. These are small DRACO-style tasks for an explicit `open-fusion-eval --suite fusion --demo` entry point; they are not a substitute for the full hosted OpenRouter Fusion chatroom or DRACO benchmark.

tasks.prompt: Two reviewers disagree about whether a startup should adopt microservices now. Produce a decision memo that reconciles the disagreement, names the decisive trade-offs, calls out any missing evidence, and ends with a recommendation.

rubric.description: Identifies shared ground instead of treating views as wholly incompatible
Explicitly adjudicates the contradiction between speed/simplicity and scalability/independence
Calls out missing evidence or blind spots
Includes at least one reference URL when evidence is cited
PENALTY: claims microservices are always superior or always required

demo.panel: Reviewer A favors a modular monolith for speed and lower operational overhead. Reviewer B favors microservices for team autonomy and independent scaling.
demo.fused:
Decision memo
- Common ground: both reviewers care about shipping quickly without blocking future scale.
- Where they differ / trade-off: microservices can help independent scaling and team autonomy, but they add distributed-systems and operational overhead before the startup has enough traffic or team boundaries to justify it.
- Blind spot / missing evidence: current traffic, team size, deployment maturity, and expected service boundaries are unknown.
- Recommendation: start with a well-modularized monolith, instrument boundaries, and split services only when scaling or ownership evidence appears.
Reference: https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html
demo.baseline: Microservices are modern and always superior. The startup should use microservices immediately because scale matters.

tasks.prompt: Given competing analyses of whether to use RAG or fine-tuning for a customer-support assistant, synthesize the best answer. Preserve unique insights, identify partial coverage, note blind spots, and recommend a path.

rubric.description: Correctly explains RAG is useful for changing/external knowledge
Correctly explains fine-tuning is useful for style, format, or stable behavior
Preserves a unique insight from one perspective
Notes partial coverage or aspects only some analyses considered
Mentions blind spots or missing evaluation data
PENALTY: presents RAG vs fine-tuning as an unconditional binary

demo.panel: RAG handles fresh knowledge; fine-tuning controls style and stable behavior. Evaluate on held-out support tickets.
demo.fused:
Recommendation: use RAG first for support knowledge, then fine-tune only if style or stable behavior remains poor.
- RAG is best for changing or external knowledge because retrieval can update without retraining.
- Fine-tuning is useful for style, answer format, tone, and stable behavior.
- Unique insight to preserve: evaluate on a holdout set of real support tickets before paying for tuning.
- Partial coverage: one analysis may focus on retrieval quality while another focuses on response consistency; both matter.
- Blind spot: missing data on knowledge-base freshness, ticket distribution, and acceptable hallucination rate.
Reference: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/retrieval
demo.baseline: Always choose RAG and never fine-tune. RAG is retrieval.
